transcript.transcript: Uh, record. Uh, this is Doctor Fiona Vernal on the 25th of October, 2021, conducting an oral history with Mr. Sanford Cloud. If you could provide your oral consent for this, um, oral history, I would really appreciate it. Yes. You have my consent. All right, so I asked if you could give me a sense of what Georgia meant to your family. Is it like the old country, the way it is, the way that Jamaica or Italy might be for for generations of immigrants? Um, I don't think there's any. Um. Hold on. There's something on my screen says the meeting is being recorded and it's blocking out your face. So let me just put continue. Okay. Here we go. Okay, okay. Um, I don't think it has any significant meaning, uh, Fiona. Only because my, um. My father's family. Um. Came to Connecticut, uh, in like 19, 19, 1920, when he was just ten years old. Right. And my mother, I don't even I don't even remember how old she would have been. But I believe that she was also a child. So there really wasn't that much conversation as I was growing up. Um, about, uh, George, obviously my my father's mother and father, uh, would have the memories, but. And my dad was the oldest of 11 children, so, um, uh, he would be the only one that would have had any kind of, Um, you know, memory or feeling about, uh, about that. But, uh, uh, it's not anything that we ever, we ever talked about as I, as I was growing up. Have, um, have have you or your siblings gone back to your your, um, your your parents, um, place of birth to check it out as their place of birth? No. No, no. Oh, right. Okay. So I think okay, so that that clarifies that question. And when you talked about your grandfather relocating, was it from the South Windsor, um, area where he was farming before and coming to her? Yes, yes. That's correct. What what was. That? I don't know, it was I think it was called East Windsor Hill at the time. East Windsor hill? Yeah. Um, how did he end up there in the first place? I have no idea. Well, I guess it was farmland because it was farmland, right. And he, you know, he was a farmer at the at the time. And, um, but other than that, I that's that's the only recollection that I have. Um, and do you know what attracted him? Oh. I'm sorry. What attracted him to Hartford? Oh, my my my guess is, um, my guess is a better a better set of job opportunities, I think. I think he ended up leaving farming and worked, uh, in, in other, um, in other industries. And can you tell me a little bit about what Hartford, um, was like by the time you, you would you were coming of age. So maybe by the time you're 12, 13, 14, 15. What kind of impression? Um, was Hartford, um, making on you? Did you feel like you were in a big city? Did you have a good sense of where Hartford, like fit in relation to other places. Um, I just I remember Hartford as a very, um, nice community in which to, to grow up at during that, that period of time. Um, and, uh, and, and we, we moved uh, we or I guess the first several years of my life, we lived on the off of Park Street, uh, on a street called Wolcott Street. Um, and that would have been in South Hartford. And, uh, uh, what I remember about that is that as a, as a youngster, um, uh, attending, uh, kindergarten and first grade and second grade, Um, I used, I remembered well, uh, going to the library, uh, which was just up the street off of, um, Park. Um, Park Street. Um, that was my, um. Uh. My big adventure, uh, walking 3 or 4 blocks up to the library. Um, you know, by myself at five, six, six years old. Um, I have a little memory of, um, my mother taking me. My brother. Um. And my, uh, my sister to, um, Bushnell Park from time to time. Um. Which, uh, was a was a pretty good, good walk from, from Walcott Street. And then in 1952, uh, we moved as a family from Walcott Street to a brand new, um, public housing development called Westbrook Village, uh, which, um, sat off of, um, all Albany Avenue, uh, uh, just below, uh, just below homestead, uh, Homestead Avenue. And, um, it was a, uh, I recall a two bedroom. Excuse me, Mr. Cloud. One. Excuse me one second. I'm going to pause the recording. My car alarm is going off. I have to go. Stop it. Hold on one second. All right. Okay. One second. All right.

lines.line: # Oral Consent and Introduction

> On October 25, 2021, Doctor Fiona Vernal begins an oral history interview with Mr. Sanford Cloud. She requests oral consent for the interview, which Mr. Cloud provides, allowing the conversation to proceed.

metadata.title: Oral Consent and Introduction
metadata.synopsis: On October 25, 2021, Doctor Fiona Vernal begins an oral history interview with Mr. Sanford Cloud. She requests oral consent for the interview, which Mr. Cloud provides, allowing the conversation to proceed.

lines.line: # Family Connection to Georgia

> The speaker is asked about the significance of Georgia to their family. They explain that Georgia does not hold any particular meaning for them, as their parents moved to Connecticut when they were children, and there was little conversation about Georgia during their upbringing.

metadata.title: Family Connection to Georgia
metadata.synopsis: The speaker is asked about the significance of Georgia to their family. They explain that Georgia does not hold any particular meaning for them, as their parents moved to Connecticut when they were children, and there was little conversation about Georgia during their upbringing.

lines.line: # Technical Interruption

> The speaker encounters a technical issue when a notification about the meeting being recorded appears on their screen, obscuring Doctor Vernal's face. They pause to address the issue by clicking continue.

metadata.title: Technical Interruption
metadata.synopsis: The speaker encounters a technical issue when a notification about the meeting being recorded appears on their screen, obscuring Doctor Vernal's face. They pause to address the issue by clicking continue.

lines.line: # Family's Move to Connecticut

> The speaker elaborates on their family's history, noting that their father's family moved to Connecticut around 1919 or 1920 when their father was ten years old. They mention that their father was the oldest of 11 children and would be the only one with any memories of Georgia, but it was not a topic of discussion during their childhood.

metadata.title: Family's Move to Connecticut
metadata.synopsis: The speaker elaborates on their family's history, noting that their father's family moved to Connecticut around 1919 or 1920 when their father was ten years old. They mention that their father was the oldest of 11 children and would be the only one with any memories of Georgia, but it was not a topic of discussion during their childhood.

lines.line: # Grandfather's Relocation to Hartford

> The speaker confirms that neither they nor their siblings have visited their parents' birthplace. They discuss their grandfather's relocation from South Windsor, where he was a farmer, to Hartford, which they speculate was due to better job opportunities.

metadata.title: Grandfather's Relocation to Hartford
metadata.synopsis: The speaker confirms that neither they nor their siblings have visited their parents' birthplace. They discuss their grandfather's relocation from South Windsor, where he was a farmer, to Hartford, which they speculate was due to better job opportunities.

lines.line: # Impressions of Hartford

> The speaker shares their childhood memories of Hartford, describing it as a nice community. They recall living off of Park Street, visiting the library, and going to Bushnell Park with their family. They also mention moving to a new public housing development called Westbrook Village in 1952.

metadata.title: Impressions of Hartford
metadata.synopsis: The speaker shares their childhood memories of Hartford, describing it as a nice community. They recall living off of Park Street, visiting the library, and going to Bushnell Park with their family. They also mention moving to a new public housing development called Westbrook Village in 1952.

lines.line: # Interruption Due to Car Alarm
> The interview is momentarily interrupted when Doctor Vernal hears her car alarm going off. She pauses the recording to address the situation, indicating that she needs to stop the alarm.

metadata.title: Interruption Due to Car Alarm
metadata.synopsis: The interview is momentarily interrupted when Doctor Vernal hears her car alarm going off. She pauses the recording to address the situation, indicating that she needs to stop the alarm.

# Family Connection to Georgia

# Technical Interruption

# Family's Move to Connecticut

They mention that their father was the oldest of 11 children and would be the only one with any memories of Georgia, but it was not a topic of discussion during their childhood.
# Grandfather's Relocation to Hartford

> The speaker confirms that neither they nor their siblings have visited their parents' birthplace. They discuss their grandfather's relocation from South Windsor, where he was a farmer, to Hartford, which they speculate was due to better job opportunities.

lines.line: # Consent and Introduction

> The interviewer, Dr. Fiona Vernal, begins the oral history session by formally requesting consent from Mr. Sanford Cloud. The speaker provides his consent, establishing the ethical foundation for the interview. This section sets the stage for the conversation and introduces the participants and the date of the interview.

metadata.title: Consent and Introduction
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer, Dr. Fiona Vernal, begins the oral history session by formally requesting consent from Mr. Sanford Cloud. The speaker provides his consent, establishing the ethical foundation for the interview. This section sets the stage for the conversation and introduces the participants and the date of the interview.

lines.line: # Georgia's Significance to the Family

> The interviewer asks about the cultural and emotional significance of Georgia to the speaker's family, comparing it to the 'old country' for other immigrant groups. The speaker explains that Georgia does not hold a special meaning for his family, primarily because his father's family moved to Connecticut when his father was very young. The lack of strong ties or memories is emphasized, as Georgia was not a frequent topic of conversation during his upbringing.

metadata.title: Georgia's Significance to the Family
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer asks about the cultural and emotional significance of Georgia to the speaker's family, comparing it to the 'old country' for other immigrant groups. The speaker explains that Georgia does not hold a special meaning for his family, primarily because his father's family moved to Connecticut when his father was very young. The lack of strong ties or memories is emphasized, as Georgia was not a frequent topic of conversation during his upbringing.

lines.line: # Family Migration and Memories of Georgia

> The speaker continues to discuss the family's migration from Georgia, noting that both his parents were children when they moved. He reflects on the absence of discussions about Georgia in his family, suggesting that only his grandparents and possibly his father, as the eldest child, would have had any memories. The interviewer asks whether the speaker or his siblings have ever visited their parents' birthplace, and the speaker confirms that they have not, further illustrating the lack of connection to Georgia.

metadata.title: Family Migration and Memories of Georgia
metadata.synopsis: The speaker continues to discuss the family's migration from Georgia, noting that both his parents were children when they moved. He reflects on the absence of discussions about Georgia in his family, suggesting that only his grandparents and possibly his father, as the eldest child, would have had any memories. The interviewer asks whether the speaker or his siblings have ever visited their parents' birthplace, and the speaker confirms that they have not, further illustrating the lack of connection to Georgia.

lines.line: # Grandfather's Relocation and Farming in Connecticut

> The interviewer shifts focus to the speaker's grandfather, asking about his move from South Windsor/East Windsor Hill and his farming background. The speaker recalls that his grandfather was a farmer and likely chose the area because it was farmland. He admits to not knowing the exact reasons for the move but speculates that Hartford offered better job opportunities, prompting his grandfather to leave farming for other industries.

metadata.title: Grandfather's Relocation and Farming in Connecticut
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer shifts focus to the speaker's grandfather, asking about his move from South Windsor/East Windsor Hill and his farming background. The speaker recalls that his grandfather was a farmer and likely chose the area because it was farmland. He admits to not knowing the exact reasons for the move but speculates that Hartford offered better job opportunities, prompting his grandfather to leave farming for other industries.

lines.line: # Impressions of Hartford During Childhood

> The interviewer asks about the speaker's experiences growing up in Hartford. The speaker describes Hartford as a pleasant community during his youth, recalling his early years living on Wolcott Street in South Hartford. He shares memories of attending school, visiting the local library, and walking to Bushnell Park with his family. In 1952, the family moved to Westbrook Village, a new public housing development off Albany Avenue, marking a significant change in their living situation.

lines.line: # Interruption Due to Car Alarm

> The interviewer interrupts the session because her car alarm is going off, indicating a pause in the recording. This brief section marks a break in the conversation and signals a temporary halt to the oral history interview.

story.title: Sanford cloud part 1
story.description: The interview conducted by Dr. Fiona Vernal with Mr. Sanford Cloud discusses the family's migration history from Georgia to Connecticut and their lack of cultural ties to their roots in Georgia. Mr. Cloud reflects on his childhood in Hartford, mentioning the move from South Hartford to Westbrook Village in a new public housing development in 1952. He remembers Hartford as a nice community during his early years, highlighting personal experiences like walking to the library and visiting Bushnell Park. The move to Hartford was driven by better job opportunities as his grandfather transitioned from farming to other industries.
